claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 4 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 16 years old and does not live with you.
  - Charlie is 15 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 4 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 12 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 16 and in the 20-week extension period
  - Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 15 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility properly, I'll need to ask you some questions.

First, let me review the official eligibility rules to ensure I give you accurate guidance.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for waiting. Now I can help you assess your eligibility for Child Benefit.

To start, could you please tell me: **Do you have any children, and if so, how many?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have three children actually. Alex is 4, Charlie is 15, and Blake is 16.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So you have three children: Alex (4), Charlie (15), and Blake (16).

Now I need to ask you some questions about each child to assess eligibility properly.

Let me start with a general question: **Do all three children (Alex, Charlie, and Blake) currently live with you in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I live in the UK, yes. Alex and Charlie live with me at home, but Blake doesn't live with me anymore.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. So Alex and Charlie live with you in the UK, but Blake doesn't live with you anymore.

Let me ask about Blake first: **When did Blake stop living with you, and are you making any financial contributions towards Blake's upkeep (such as money for clothes, food, pocket money, or helping with accommodation costs)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake moved out fairly recently. I do help out financially though - I contribute towards their upkeep each week for things like clothes and food.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's helpful to know. Just to clarify: **How much do you contribute towards Blake's upkeep each week?** (This is important because to continue receiving Child Benefit when a child lives elsewhere, you need to contribute at least the same amount as the Child Benefit payment or the equivalent in kind.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I contribute £50 a week towards Blake's upkeep.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. That's very helpful information.

Now, regarding Blake who is 16: **Is Blake currently in approved education or training?** (For example, studying at school, college, or doing an approved training course?)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake actually left school recently, but they've just signed up with the local careers service to figure out what they want to do next.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's useful information. Just to clarify the timing: **How long ago did Blake leave education, and when did they register with the careers service?** (This matters because Child Benefit can continue for up to 20 weeks if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education and registers with a government-sponsored careers service.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: It's been quite recent - they left school and registered with the careers service within the last few months. They're still within that period where they're figuring things out.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that information. Now let me ask about Charlie, who is 15: **Is Charlie in education or training?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Charlie is still in school - they're 15, so they're still at that age where they have to be in education.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, thank you. And finally, regarding Alex who is 4: **Is there anything unusual about Alex's situation - for example, are they in hospital for an extended period, in care, or living with someone else? Or do they live with you full-time at home?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex lives with me, but they have been in hospital for a while now - about three months actually. They've needed medical treatment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'm sorry to hear Alex has been in hospital. I hope they're doing okay.

Just to understand the situation fully: **Have you been regularly spending money on Alex while they've been in hospital?** (For example, on things like toys, clothes, treats, or other items for them?)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I have been - I still buy things for Alex like clothes, their favorite snacks, and little treats to keep their spirits up while they're in there.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for providing all that information. Let me also check one final thing: **Is anyone else claiming Child Benefit for any of these three children?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for any of them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Good news! You are eligible for Child Benefit for all three of your children.**

Here's the breakdown for each child:

**Alex (age 4):** ✅ **Eligible**
- Alex is under 16 and lives with you in the UK
- Although they've been in hospital for about 12 weeks, you continue to regularly spend money on them (clothes, snacks, and treats)
- The rules allow Child Benefit to continue after 12 weeks in hospital if you're regularly spending money on the child, which you are

**Charlie (age 15):** ✅ **Eligible**  
- Charlie is under 16, lives with you in the UK, and is still in school
- You meet all the standard eligibility criteria for Charlie

**Blake (age 16):** ✅ **Eligible**
- Blake recently left education but registered with the careers service within the last few months
- You can receive Child Benefit for up to 20 weeks when a 16 or 17 year old leaves education and registers with a government-sponsored careers service
- Even though Blake no longer lives with you, you're contributing £50 per week towards their upkeep, which is at least equivalent to the Child Benefit payment amount
- This qualifies you to continue receiving Child Benefit for Blake during this 20-week period

Since nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for any of your children, you should be able to claim (or continue claiming) for all three.

reasonings: Alex is 4 years old (under 16) and lives with you in the UK. Although Alex has been in hospital for approximately 3 months (12 weeks), you continue to regularly spend money on them (clothes, snacks, treats). According to the rules, after 12 weeks in hospital, you can still be entitled to Child Benefit if you regularly spend money on the child. Since you are actively doing this and nobody else is claiming for Alex, you remain eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Charlie is 15 years old (under 16), lives with you in the UK, and is still in school. You are responsible for Charlie as they live with you. Nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Charlie. You meet all the standard eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for Charlie.

Blake is 16 years old and recently left education but has registered with the local careers service within the last few months. According to the rules, you can continue to receive Child Benefit for up to 20 weeks if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education and registers with a government-sponsored careers service. Although Blake no longer lives with you, you are contributing £50 per week towards their upkeep (clothes, food, etc.), which is at least equivalent to the Child Benefit payment amount. According to the rules, you can continue to receive Child Benefit when a child lives elsewhere if you contribute at least the same amount as the Child Benefit payment towards their upkeep. Nobody else is claiming for Blake. Therefore, you are eligible for Child Benefit for Blake during this 20-week period following their registration with the careers service.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK and has three children: Alex (4), Charlie (15), and Blake (16). Nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for any of the children. Alex and Charlie live with the claimant, and Blake lives elsewhere but the claimant contributes £50 weekly towards Blake's upkeep. Alex has been in hospital for about 12 weeks but the claimant regularly spends money on them, which allows continued eligibility. Charlie is under 16 and in school, meeting standard eligibility criteria. Blake is 16, recently left education, and registered with a careers service within the qualifying 20-week period, and the claimant makes sufficient financial contributions despite Blake living elsewhere. The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for all three children.

Although Alex has been in hospital for approximately 3 months (12 weeks), you continue to regularly spend money on them (clothes, snacks, treats). According to the rules, after 12 weeks in hospital, you can still be entitled to Child Benefit if you regularly spend money on the child. Since you are actively doing this and nobody else is claiming for Alex, you remain eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Charlie is 15 years old (under 16), lives with you in the UK, and is still in school. You are responsible for Charlie as they live with you. Nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Charlie. You meet all the standard eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for Charlie.

Blake is 16 years old and recently left education but has registered with the local careers service within the last few months. According to the rules, you can continue to receive Child Benefit for up to 20 weeks if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education and registers with a government-sponsored careers service. Although Blake no longer lives with you, you are contributing £50 per week towards their upkeep (clothes, food, etc.), which is at least equivalent to the Child Benefit payment amount. According to the rules, you can continue to receive Child Benefit when a child lives elsewhere if you contribute at least the same amount as the Child Benefit payment towards their upkeep. Nobody else is claiming for Blake. Therefore, you are eligible for Child Benefit for Blake during this 20-week period following their registration with the careers service.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK and has three children: Alex (4), Charlie (15), and Blake (16). Nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for any of the children. Alex and Charlie live with the claimant, and Blake lives elsewhere but the claimant contributes £50 weekly towards Blake's upkeep. Alex has been in hospital for about 12 weeks but the claimant regularly spends money on them, which allows continued eligibility. Charlie is under 16 and in school, meeting standard eligibility criteria. Blake is 16, recently left education, and registered with a careers service within the qualifying 20-week period, and the claimant makes sufficient financial contributions despite Blake living elsewhere. The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for all three children.
